title: On the existence of fully inseparable biseparable Gaussian states
authors: Olga Leskovjanová, Klára Baksová, Jan Provazník, Ladislav Mišta,, Nicolai Friis
abstract: Genuine multipartite entanglement and full inseparability are two inequivalent quantum resources. Even though all genuinely multipartite entangled states are also fully inseparable, not all fully inseparable states are genuinely multipartite entangled. There exist fully inseparable states that can be prepared as convex mixtures of states separable with respect to different bipartite splits. Here, we are interested in examples of Gaussian states that possess this type of entanglement, so-called fully inseparable biseparable states. We show for several archetypical families of multimode Gaussian states that fully inseparable biseparable candidate states are actually genuinely multipartite entangled. Using projections to finite-dimensional subspaces and fully decomposable witnesses, we observe a shrinking of the regions of potentially fully inseparable biseparable Gaussian states with growing dimension of the projection subspaces. We therefore conjecture that all fully inseparable Gaussian states are genuinely multipartite entangled.
summary: The paper explores whether Gaussian states can be fully inseparable without being genuinely multipartite entangled. By analyzing various three-mode Gaussian families using local projections to finite-dimensional subspaces, the authors find no evidence for such states and conjecture that all fully inseparable Gaussian states are genuinely multipartite entangled.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether 'fully inseparable biseparable' (FIB) Gaussian states exist, specifically whether there are Gaussian states that are entangled across every bipartition but are still expressible as a convex mixture of states separable with respect to different partitions.
main_result: The authors find that for all tested families of three-mode Gaussian states, states appearing to be FIB are actually genuinely multipartite entangled (GME), leading to the conjecture that all fully inseparable Gaussian states are GME.
method: The study uses the PPT criterion for entanglement detection, projections of Gaussian density matrices onto finite-dimensional Fock subspaces, and semidefinite programming (SDP) to construct fully decomposable witnesses.
model_or_system: The study focuses on three-mode continuous-variable Gaussian states, including families constructed from mixtures of two-mode squeezed vacuum states with single-mode squeezed, thermal, or coherent states, as well as noisy GHZ-like Gaussian states.
key_observables: Squeezing parameter (r), mean photon number (n), displacement (alpha), and noise/mixing parameter (eta).
important_parameters: The dimension of the local projection subspaces (d=2, 3, 4) and the squeezing parameter (r).
main_assumptions: The study is restricted to three-mode Gaussian states and relies on the observation that the gap between full inseparability and GME shrinks with increasing projection dimension to form a conjecture.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the hierarchy of multipartite entanglement; Figures 2-6 show detection ranges for various Gaussian state families (SMSV, thermal, coherent, and noisy GHZ) across different detection methods and subspace dimensions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the distinction between full inseparability and GME, defines the Gaussian state models, describes the numerical methods for Fock-basis projection, presents results for various state families, and concludes with a conjecture and discussion of implications.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant to quantum optics and continuous-variable quantum information as it addresses a fundamental question about the structure of entanglement in Gaussian states and suggests that standard covariance matrix-based tests might be sufficient for GME detection.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Graphical representation of sets of tripartite states with different separability properties. The convex hull of partition-separable states (II) forms the set of biseparable states, and its complement to the whole set of states is com- posed of states with GME (IV). The complement to the set of partition-separable states within the set of biseparable states is the set of FIB states (III). The union of the sets (III) and (IV) forms the set of fully inseparable states.

FIG. 3. Comparison of ranges of the squeezing parameter r for which the Gaussian state with CM γ (1) ABC is detected as fully inseparable (blue, bottom), and as genuinely multipartite en- tangled using different detection methods (top to bottom): the witness inequality (10), or the fully decomposable witness in the form of the SDP in (8) after local projection to three qubits (green, second from the top), to three qutrits (red, third from the top), and to three ququarts (yellow, fourth from the top).

FIG. 2. Comparison of ranges of the threshold squeezing pa- rameter r for which the Gaussian state with CM γ (0) ABC is de- tected as fully inseparable (blue, bottom), and as genuinely multipartite entangled using different detection methods (top to bottom): the witness inequality (10), or the fully decom- posable witness in the form of the SDP in (8) after local pro- jection to three qubits (green, second from the top) [31], to three qutrits (red, third from the top), and to three ququarts (yellow, fourth from the top).

FIG. 5. Comparison of regions where the Gaussian state with CM γ (3) ABC, Eq. (19), is detected as GME by the witness in- equality (10) (I), and by the fully decomposable witness W on qubits (II), qutrits (III), and ququarts (IV), inside the fully- inseparable region (V).

FIG. 4. Comparison of regions where the Gaussian state with CM γ (2) ABC is detected as GME by the witness inequality (10) (I), and by the fully decomposable witness W on qubits (II), qutrits (III), and ququarts (IV), inside the fully-inseparable region (V).
